volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Trinummus (Section 3.1.607c - Section 3.3.745)

translation.label: Scaife English translation
translation.citation: Trinummus: The Three Pieces of Money

sections.label: Section 3.1.607c
sections.original: Me nihili pendere .
sections.translation: That I don ’ t care a fig for your belief .

sections.label: Section 3.1.608
sections.original: Quam dudum istuc aut ubi actumst ?
sections.translation: How long since , or where , was this matter agreed to ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.608b
sections.original: Ilico hic ante ostium ,
sections.translation: On this very spot — here , before his door ( pointing to PHILTO ’ S house ) . This moment-like [*] , as the man of Praeneste says .

sections.label: Section 3.1.609b
sections.original: Tanton in re perdita ,
sections.translation: And has Lesbonicus , amid his ruined fortunes , become so much more frugal than in his prosperous circumstances ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.611
sections.original: Atque equidem ipsus ultro venit Philto oratum filio .
sections.translation: Why , in fact , Philto himself came of his own accord to make the offer for his son .

sections.label: Section 3.1.612
sections.original: Flagitium quidem hercle fiet , nisi dos dabitur virgini .
sections.translation: By my troth , it really will be a disgrace , if a portion is not given to the maiden . In fine , I think , i ’ faith , that that matter concerns myself . I will go to my corrector , and will ask advice of him .

sections.label: Section 3.1.615
sections.original: Propemodum quid illic festinet sentio et subolet mihi :
sections.translation: I pretty nearly guess , and I have a strong suspicion , why he makes such speed on this : namely , that he may turn Lesbonicus out of his bit of land , after he has turned him out of his house . O Charmides , my master ! since your property here is being torn to pieces in your absence , I wish I could see you return safe , that you might both take vengeance on your enemies , and give the reward to me according as I have behaved , and do behave towards you .

sections.label: Section 3.1.620
sections.original: nimium difficilest reperiri amicum ita ut nomen cluet ,
sections.translation: ’ Tis an extremely difficult thing for a friend to be found really such as the name imports , to whom , when you have entrusted your interests , you may sleep without any care . But lo ! I perceive our son-in-law [*] coming , together with his neighbour . Something — what , I know not — is wrong between them . They are walking , each with a hasty step ; the one is catching the other that is before him by the cloak .

sections.label: Section 3.1.625
sections.original: haud ineuscheme astiterunt . huc aliquantum apscessero :
sections.translation: They have come to a stop in no very courteous fashion . I ’ ll step aside here a little distance . I have a wish to hear the conversation of these two that are to be connected by marriage .

sections.label: Section 3.2.627
sections.original: Sta ilico , noli avorsari neque te occultassis mihi .
sections.translation: Stay , this moment ; don ’ t turn away , and don ’ t hide yourself from me .

sections.label: Section 3.2.628
sections.original: Potin ut me ire quo profectus sum sinas ?
sections.translation: Can ’ t you allow me to go whither I was proceeding ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.628b
sections.original: Si in rem tuam ,
sections.translation: If , Lesbonicus , it seems to be to your interest , either for your glory or for your honour , I will let you go .

sections.label: Section 3.2.630
sections.original: Quod est facillumum , facís .
sections.translation: You are doing a thing that it is very easy to do .

sections.label: Section 3.2.630b
sections.original: Quid id est ?
sections.translation: What is that ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.630c
sections.original: Amico iniuriam .
sections.translation: An injury to a friend .

sections.label: Section 3.2.631
sections.original: Neque meumst neque facere didici .
sections.translation: It is no way of mine , and I have not learned so to do .

sections.label: Section 3.2.631b
sections.original: Indoctus quam docte facis .
sections.translation: Untaught as you are , how cleverly you do it . What would you have done , if any one had taught you to be thus annoying to me ? You , who , when you pretend to be acting kindly to me , use me ill , and are intending evil .

sections.label: Section 3.2.634
sections.original: Egone ?
sections.translation: What !— I ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.634b
sections.original: Tune .
sections.translation: Yes — you .

sections.label: Section 3.2.634c
sections.original: Quid male facio ?
sections.translation: How do I use you ill ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.634d
sections.original: Quod ego nolo , id cum facis .
sections.translation: Inasmuch as you do that which I do not wish .

sections.label: Section 3.2.635
sections.original: Tuae rei bene consulere cupio .
sections.translation: I wish to consult your advantage .

sections.label: Section 3.2.635b
sections.original: Tu mihi es melior quam egomet mihi ?
sections.translation: Are you kinder to me than I am to myself ? I have sense enough ; I see sufficiently well those things that are for my own advantage .

sections.label: Section 3.2.637
sections.original: An id est sapere , ut qui beneficium a benevolente repudies ?
sections.translation: And is it having sense enough to refuse a kindness from a well-wisher ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.638
sections.original: Nullum beneficium ésse duco id quod quoi facias non placet .
sections.translation: I reckon it to be no kindness , when it does not please him on whom you are conferring it . I know , and I understand myself what I am doing , and my mind forsakes not its duty ;

sections.label: Section 3.2.640
sections.original: nec tuis depellar dictis quin rumori serviam .
sections.translation: nor will I be driven by your speeches from paying due regard to my own character .

sections.label: Section 3.2.641
sections.original: Quid ais ? nam retineri nequeo quin dicam ea quae promeres :
sections.translation: What do you say ? For now I cannot be restrained from saying to you the things which you deserve . Have your forefathers , I pray , so handed down this reputation to you , that you , by your excesses , might lose what before was gained by their merit , and that you might become a bar to the honour of your own posterity ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.645
sections.original: tibi paterque avosque facilem fecit et planam viam
sections.translation: Your father and your grandfather made an easy and a level path for you to attain to honour ; whereas you have made it to become a difficult one , by your extreme recklessness and sloth , and your besotted ways . You have made your election , to prefer your passions to virtue . Now , do you suppose that you can cover over your faults by these means ? Alas ! ’ tis impossible .

sections.label: Section 3.2.650
sections.original: cape sis virtutem animo et corde expelle desidiam tuo :
sections.translation: Welcome virtue to your mind , if you please , and expel slothfulness from your heart . Give your attention to your he-friends in the Courts of justice [*] , and not to the couch of your she-friend , as you are wont to do . And earnestly do I now wish this piece of land to be left to you for this reason , that you may have wherewithal to reform yourself ; so that those citizens , whom you have for enemies , may not be able altogether to throw your poverty in your teeth .

sections.label: Section 3.2.655
sections.original: Omnia ego istaec quae tu dixti scio , vel exsignavero ,
sections.translation: All these things which you have been saying , I know — could even set my seal [*] to them : how I have spoiled my patrimonial estate and the fair fame of my forefathers . I knew how it became me to live ; to my misfortune I was not able to act accordingly . Thus , overpowered by the force of passion , inclined to ease , I fell into the snare ; and now to you , quite as you deserve , I do return most hearty thanks .

sections.label: Section 3.2.660
sections.original: At operam perire meam sic et te haec dicta corde spernere †
sections.translation: Still , I cannot suffer my labour to be thus lost , and yourself to despise these words ; at the same time , it grieves me that you have so little shame . And , in fine , unless you listen to me , and do this that I mention , you yourself will easily lie concealed behind your own self , so that honour cannot find you ; when you will wish yourself to be especially distinguished , you will be lying in obscurity .

sections.label: Section 3.2.665
sections.original: pernovi equidem , Lesbonice , ingenium tuom ingenuom admodum ;
sections.translation: I know right well , for my part , Lesbonicus , your highly ingenuous disposition ; I know that of your own accord you have not done wrong , but that it is Love that has blinded your heart ; and I myself comprehend all the ways of Love . As the charge of the balista [*] is hurled , so is Love ; nothing is there so swift , or that so swiftly flies ; he , too , makes the manners of men both foolish and froward [*] .

sections.label: Line 670
sections.original: minus placet magis quód suadetur , quod dissuadetur placet ;
sections.translation: That which is the most commended pleases him the least [*] ; that from which he is dissuaded pleases him . When there is a scarcity , then you long for a thing ; when there is an abundance of it , then you don ’ t care for it . The person that warns him off from a thing , the same invites him ; he that persuades him to it interdicts him . ’ Tis a misfortune of insanity for you to fly to Cupid for refuge . But I advise you again and again to think of this , how you should seek to act .

sections.label: Section 3.2.675
sections.original: si istuc , ut conare , facis † indicium tuom incendes genus ;
sections.translation: If you attempt to do according as you are now showing signs [*] , you will cause the conflagration of your family ; and then , in consequence , you will have a desire for water with which to quench this conflagration of your family . And if you should obtain it , just as lovers are subtle in their devices , you will not leave even one spark with which your family may brighten up .

sections.label: Section 3.2.679
sections.original: Facilest inventu : datur ignis , tametsi ab inimico petas .
sections.translation: ’ Tis easy to be found : fire is granted , even though you should ask it of a foe .

sections.label: Section 3.2.680
sections.original: sed tu obiurgans me a peccatis rapis deteriorem in viam .
sections.translation: But you , by your reproof , are urging me from my faults to a viler course . You are persuading me to give you my sister without a portion . But it does not become me , who have misused so great a patrimony , to be still in affluent circumstances , and to be possessing land , but her to be in want , so as with good reason to detest me . Never will he be respected by others who makes himself despised by his own relatives .

sections.label: Section 3.2.685
sections.original: sicut dixi , faciam : nolo te iactari diutius .
sections.translation: As I said , I will do ; I do not wish you to be in doubt any longer .

sections.label: Section 3.2.686
sections.original: Tanto meliust te sororis causa egestatem exsequi
sections.translation: And is it so much preferable that for your sister ’ s sake you should incur poverty , and that I should possess that piece of land rather than yourself , who ought to be upholding your own walls ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.688
sections.original: Nolo ego mihi te tam prospicere , qui meam egestatem leves ,
sections.translation: I do not wish you so much to have regard to myself , in order that you may relieve my poverty , as that in my neediness I may not become disgraced : that people may not spread about this report of me ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.690
sections.original: me germanam meam sororem in concubinatum tibi ,
sections.translation: that I gave my own sister without a portion to you , rather in concubinage [*] than in marriage . Who would be said to be more dishonorable than I ? The spreading of this report might do credit to you , but it would defile me , if you were to marry her without a portion . For you it would be a gain of reputation , for me it would be something for people to throw in my teeth .

sections.label: Section 3.2.695
sections.original: Quid ? te dictatorem censes fore , si aps té agrum acceperim ?
sections.translation: Why so ? Do you suppose [*] that you will become Dictator if I accept the land of you ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.696
sections.original: Neque volo neque postulo neque censeo , verum tamen
sections.translation: I neither wish , nor require , nor do I think so ; but still , to be mindful of his duty , is true honour to an upright man .

sections.label: Section 3.2.698
sections.original: Scio equidem te animatus ut sis ; video , subolet , sentio :
sections.translation: For my part , I know you , how you are disposed in mind ; I see it , I discover it , I apprehend . You are doing this , that when you have formed an alliance between us ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.700
sections.original: atque eum agrum dederis nec quicquam hic tibi sit qui vitam colas ,
sections.translation: and when you have given up this piece of land , and have nothing here with which to support life , in beggary you may fly from the city , in exile you may desert your country , your kindred , your connexions , your friends ,— the nuptials once over . People would suppose that you were frightened hence by my means , and through my cupidity . Do not fancy in your mind that I will act so as to allow that to happen .

sections.label: Section 3.2.705
sections.original: Non enim póssum quin exclamem euge . euge , Lysiteles , πάλιν .
sections.translation: Well , I cannot but exclaim , Well done , well done , Lysiteles , encore [*] . Easily do you win the victory ; the other is conquered : your performance is superior . This one ( pointing to LYSITELES ) acts better in character , and composes better lines [*] . By reason of your folly do you still dispute it ? Stand in awe of the fine .

sections.label: Section 3.2.709
sections.original: Quid tibi intérpellatio aut in consilium huc accessio est ?
sections.translation: What means this interruption of yours , or your intrusion here upon our conversation ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.710
sections.original: Eodem pacto , quo huc accessi , apscessero .
sections.translation: The same way that I came here I ’ ll get me gone .

sections.label: Section 3.2.710b
sections.original: I hac mecum domum ,
sections.translation: Step this way home with me , Lysiteles ; there we will talk at length about these matters .

sections.label: Section 3.2.712
sections.original: Nil ego in occulto agere soleo . meus ut animust , eloquar :
sections.translation: I am not in the habit of doing anything in secret . Just as my feelings are I will speak out . If your sister , as I think it right , is thus given to me in marriage without a portion , and if you are not about to go away hence , that which shall be mine , the same shall be yours .

sections.label: Section 3.2.715
sections.original: sin aliter animatus es , bene quod agas eveniat tibi ,
sections.translation: But if you are minded otherwise , may that which you do turn out for you for the best . I will never be your friend on any other terms ; such is my determination .

sections.label: Section 3.2.717
sections.original: Abiit hercle ille quidem . ecquid audis , Lysiteles ? ego te volo .
sections.translation: Faith , he ’ s off . D ’ ye hear — Lysiteles ? I want you . He ’ s off as well . Stasimus , you remain alone . What am I now to do , but to buckle up my baggage and sling my buckler on my back [*] ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.720
sections.original: fulmentas iubeam suppingi soccis ? non sisti potest .
sections.translation: and order soles to be fastened [*] beneath my shoes ? There is no staying now . I see that no long time hence I shall be a soldier ’ s drudge . And when my master has thrown himself into the pay [*] of some potentate , I guess that among the greatest warriors he will prove a brave [*] — hand at running away , and that there he will capture the spoil , who-shall come to attack my master .

sections.label: Section 3.2.725
sections.original: egomet autem quom extemplo arcum mihi et pharetram et ságittas sumpsero ,
sections.translation: I myself , the moment that I shall have assumed my bow and quiver and arrows , and the helmet on my head , shall-go to sleep very quietly in my tent . I ’ ll be off to the Forum ; I ’ ll ask that talent [*] back of the person to whom I lent it six days since , that I may have some provision for the journey to carry with me .

sections.label: Section 3.3.729
sections.original: Vt mihi rem narras , Callicles , nullo modo
sections.translation: According as you relate the matter to me , Callicles , it really can by no means

sections.label: Section 3.3.730
sections.original: potest fíeri prosus quin dos detur virgini .
sections.translation: be but that a portion must be given to the girl .

sections.label: Section 3.3.731
sections.original: Namque hercle honeste fieri ferme non potest ,
sections.translation: Why , troth , it would hardly be honestly done on my part , if I were to allow her to contract a marriage without a portion , when I have her property in my possession at home . ⋯

sections.label: Section 3.3.734
sections.original: Parata dos domist ; nisi expectare vis ,
sections.translation: ⋯ A portion is ready at your house ; unless you like to wait

sections.label: Section 3.3.735
sections.original: ut eam sine dote frater nuptum conlocet .
sections.translation: until her brother has disposed of her in marriage without a portion . After that , you might go to Philto yourself , and might say that you present her with a portion , and that you do it on account of your intimacy with her father . But I dread this , lest that offer might bring you into crimination and disgrace with the public .

sections.label: Section 3.3.740
sections.original: non temere dicant te benignum virgini :
sections.translation: They would say that you were so kind to the girl not without some good reason ; that the dowry which you presented her was given you by her father ; they would think that you were portioning her out of that , and that you had not kept it safe for her just as it was given , and that you had withheld some part . Now , if you wish to await the return of Charmides ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.745
sections.original: perlongumst : huic ducendi interea abscesserit
sections.translation: the time is very long ; meanwhile , the inclination to marry her may leave this Lysiteles ; this proposal , too , is quite a first-rate one for her .
